title: Tail latency dominates user-visible performance
description: At scale a request often fans out to many backends, so the slow p99 of each component compounds and the tail, not the mean, defines what users feel.
body: Use when reasoning about why average-looking systems feel slow. A user request frequently depends on several sub-calls; the probability that at least one hits the slow tail grows with fan-out, so a great mean with a bad p99 still yields frequent slow requests. Do not judge a system by its average latency alone. Actions: measure and budget high percentiles, identify fan-out points, and attack the tail (timeouts, hedging, caching the slow path). Validation signals include p99/p99.9 dashboards and a fan-out latency model. Risk boundary: optimizing the mean while the tail regresses can pass naive checks yet degrade real UX — track percentiles and escalate tail regressions.
